Part 1 of the “Complete Guide to Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)” series
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Generated using GeminiIntroduction
Over the past one and a half years, I’ve been working on Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) across a variety of use cases across multiple industries and business problems. During that time, I’ve built everything from simple RAG pipelines to more advanced Agentic RAG systems, evaluated multiple embedding models, worked with several vector databases, and adapted systems to meet different production requirements.
Beyond building these systems, I have also spent a significant amount of time reading research papers, following engineering blogs, taking specialized courses offered by leading companies like NVIDIA, and learning from the broader AI community through technical discussions and YouTube. After building a few different systems, one thing became clear: there is no single “correct” way to build a RAG system. Every business problem requires a different architecture to build a scalable solution, along with different retrieval strategies that each come with their own trade-offs.
What surprised me most while learning RAG was how quickly the field changed over the past few years. When Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) was introduced in 2020, large language models were far less capable than they are today. Since then, we’ve seen remarkable progress in reasoning, instruction following, multimodal understanding, context window size, and tool use. Despite these advances, RAG has remained one of the most important techniques for building reliable AI applications. That’s because even the most powerful language models don’t know everything or always have access to the latest information. By allowing models to retrieve relevant knowledge from external sources, RAG helps reduce hallucinations and generates responses that are grounded in accurate, trustworthy information.
Today, RAG powers enterprise chatbots, coding assistants, customer support platforms, legal research tools, healthcare applications, financial systems, internal knowledge assistants, and countless other AI products.
Despite its widespread adoption, many tutorials jump directly into vector databases, embeddings, or LangChain code without answering a much more fundamental question:
Why was Retrieval-Augmented Generation invented in the first place?
That question is where this series begins.
Throughout this series, we’ll move from the original research that introduced RAG to the engineering principles behind modern production systems. Rather than focusing only on implementation, we’ll explore the reasoning behind every architectural decision, the trade-offs involved, and how these systems have evolved.
Let’s begin at the very beginning.

Why Did Large Language Models Need External Knowledge?
When models like GPT first demonstrated remarkable language understanding, many people believed they could answer almost any question.
To some extent, they could.
They learned facts about history, science, programming, mathematics, literature, and countless other topics simply by training on massive datasets. But there was a fundamental limitation.
Everything the model “knew” had been compressed into its parameters during training. Once training stopped, that knowledge became fixed.
Humans work in a similar way. Even experts don’t rely entirely on memory. A cardiologist may be highly skilled at diagnosing heart conditions, but if they encounter a rare disease or a newly approved treatment, they’ll often consult the latest clinical guidelines or medical literature before making a decision. Likewise, an experienced Python developer asked to migrate a large application to Java doesn’t magically know every Java framework or best practice to do it without degrading the user experience or performance. They’ll refer to Java documentation and choose a framework like Spring based on the application scope, migration guides, and project-specific documentation throughout the process. The expertise is already there, and the external information provides the context needed to solve the problem accurately.
Large language models face the same challenge. They have strong general knowledge, but if you ask them about an event that happened after their training cutoff, an internal company document, or your organization’s latest policies, they simply don’t have access to that information.
Even worse, instead of admitting uncertainty as humans do, language models often generate responses that sound confident but are factually incorrect, a phenomenon now widely known as hallucination.
Retraining a model every time new information became available wasn’t a practical solution. Training frontier language models requires enormous computational resources, high cost, and considerable time.
Researchers needed a way for language models to access relevant external information at the time of answering a question without retraining the model itself. That need ultimately led to the development of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and laid the foundation for many of the context engineering techniques we use today to provide language models with the right information at the right time.
That challenge became the motivation behind Retrieval-Augmented Generation.

Parametric Memory vs. Non-Parametric Memory
To understand why RAG was such a breakthrough, it’s helpful to distinguish between two types of memory.
Parametric memory refers to the knowledge stored within a model’s learned parameters. This includes patterns, concepts, language understanding, and factual information acquired during the training.
However, parametric memory has important limitations:

• It cannot be updated easily.

• It may contain outdated information.

• It can forget rare facts.

• It cannot memorize every document ever created.
RAG introduced the idea of complementing this with non-parametric memory as an external knowledge source, such as documents, databases, or an indexed corpus that can be searched at inference time.
Instead of relying solely on what the model memorized months earlier, the model can retrieve relevant information on demand before generating a response.
This simple idea fundamentally changed how AI systems could interact with evolving knowledge.

Before RAG: The Evolution of Information Retrieval
Retrieval did not begin with large language models.
Long before modern AI, search engines were solving a similar problem:
Given millions of documents, how do we find the most relevant ones for a user’s query?
Early search systems relied on lexical matching, counting how often query terms appeared in documents.
Techniques such as TF-IDF (Term Frequency–Inverse Document Frequency) improved this process by assigning greater importance to informative words while reducing the influence of common terms.
Later, BM25 became the industry standard for ranking documents, introducing a more sophisticated scoring function that considered both term frequency and document length. Even today, BM25 remains a key component of many production search systems.
These approaches worked well when the exact keywords appeared in the document. However, they struggled when two pieces of text expressed the same idea using different vocabulary. This limitation led the way for semantic retrieval.

Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR): Moving Beyond Keywords
As neural networks advanced, researchers began representing text as dense vectors, commonly known as embeddings. In simple terms, an embedding is a numerical representation of a piece of text-such as a word, sentence, or document-in a high-dimensional vector space, often called the embedding space or latent space. The individual numbers aren’t meaningful on their own. Instead, they capture the semantic meaning of the text, so pieces of text with similar meanings are positioned close together, while unrelated concepts are farther apart.
Because semantically similar text is clustered together in this space, retrieval systems no longer have to rely on exact keyword matches. Instead, they can retrieve documents based on meaning.
For example, a query like:
“How can I improve my neural network’s performance?”
could successfully retrieve a document titled:
“Techniques for optimizing deep learning models.”
Even though it doesn’t contain the exact words neural network or performance.
It soon became clear that by representing both queries and documents as dense embeddings, retrieval could be handled simply by finding the document vectors closest to the query vector. This concept became the foundation of Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR), introduced in 2020.”
DPR showed that semantic retrieval using dense embeddings could outperform traditional keyword-based retrieval on many open-domain question answering benchmarks. More importantly, it became one of the key building blocks that later enabled Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG).

Open-Domain Question Answering
While semantic retrieval was a major breakthrough, it solved only half of the problem. Retrieving relevant documents is useful, but users don’t want a list of documents; they need the exact answers.
This led to another important challenge: open-domain question answering (Open-Domain QA).
Unlike closed-domain systems, which answer questions from a fixed knowledge base or a specific dataset, open-domain QA must search across millions of documents to find relevant information before producing an answer. For example, if a user asks, “Who won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 2024?”, the system first searches a large collection of documents to identify the most relevant evidence and then uses that evidence to generate a response.
This naturally led to a simple two-stage pipeline:

• Retrieve the most relevant documents.

• Generate an answer using those retrieved documents.
If that sounds familiar, it should. This retrieve-then-generate workflow is the conceptual foundation of modern Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). The original RAG paper would later formalize this idea by combining a dense retriever with a sequence generation model into a single end-to-end architecture.

The Original RAG Paper (2020)
In 2020, researchers introduced a new framework in the paper Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks.
Their central idea was elegant:
Instead of asking a language model to answer solely from its internal parameters, first retrieve the most relevant external documents and then condition the language model on those retrieved passages during generation.
This combined the strengths of retrieval systems and sequence generation models into a single architecture.
Rather than replacing language models, retrieval enhanced them.
The result was improved factual accuracy, better performance on knowledge-intensive tasks, and the ability to update knowledge simply by changing the external document collection rather than retraining the model.
That insight laid the foundation for many of the AI systems we build today.

RAG-Sequence vs. RAG-Token
The original RAG paper proposed two different retrieval strategies: RAG-Sequence and RAG-Token.
RAG-Sequence retrieves a fixed set of relevant documents once before the language model begins generating a response. Those retrieved documents remain unchanged throughout the entire generation process. This approach is computationally efficient, simpler to implement, and continues to serve as the foundation of most production RAG systems today.
RAG-Token takes a more dynamic approach. Instead of retrieving documents only once, the model can retrieve different documents while generating different parts of the response. In theory, this allows the model to gather new information whenever additional context is needed, potentially producing more informed answers.
An easy way to think about the difference is to imagine writing a research report. With RAG-Sequence, you gather all the reference materi
